concepts.lesson: Plants perform photosynthesis to convert $CO_2$ and water into glucose using sunlight trapped by chlorophyll. This process involves three steps: absorption of light, conversion of light energy to chemical energy (splitting water), and reduction of carbon dioxide to carbohydrates.
concepts.real_example: A green leaf producing starch that can be tested with iodine (turning blue-black).
key_points: Chlorophyll is essential for light absorption
Stomata regulate gas exchange and transpiration
Oxygen is a byproduct of water splitting

concepts.lesson: Digestion is the breakdown of complex food into simpler absorbable forms. It starts in the mouth (salivary amylase), continues in the stomach (pepsin), and completes in the small intestine (trypsin, lipase). Villi in the small intestine increase surface area for absorption.
concepts.real_example: Bile juice breaking down large fat globules into smaller ones (emulsification).
key_points: Peristalsis moves food through the gut
HCl in the stomach creates an acidic medium for pepsin
Small intestine is the site of complete digestion

concepts.lesson: Respiration is the process of releasing energy from food. Aerobic respiration (in mitochondria) uses $O_2$ to break glucose into $CO_2$, water, and a large amount of ATP. Anaerobic respiration (in yeast or muscles) occurs without $O_2$, producing ethanol or lactic acid with very little energy.
concepts.real_example: Feeling muscle cramps during a fast run due to the accumulation of lactic acid.
key_points: Glucose $\rightarrow$ Pyruvate occurs in the cytoplasm
ATP is the energy currency of the cell
Terrestrial organisms use atmospheric oxygen, aquatic use dissolved oxygen

concepts.lesson: Humans have a four-chambered heart to prevent mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood. This is called double circulation. Blood travels through the lungs (pulmonary circuit) and the rest of the body (systemic circuit).
concepts.real_example: The pulse you feel in your wrist is the pressure of blood being pumped by the heart.
key_points: Arteries carry blood away from the heart (high pressure)
Veins have valves to prevent backflow
Capillaries are the site of nutrient/gas exchange

concepts.lesson: Xylem transports water and minerals upwards from roots using transpiration pull (suction) and root pressure. Phloem transports food (sucrose) from leaves to all parts of the plant using energy (ATP) in a process called translocation.
concepts.real_example: Water reaching the top of a 100-meter tall redwood tree primarily through transpiration pull.
key_points: Xylem transport is mostly passive (physical forces)
Transpiration also helps in temperature regulation

concepts.lesson: Excretion is the removal of nitrogenous wastes. In the kidney, the nephron is the functional unit. It filters blood in the glomerulus (ultrafiltration), and then useful substances like glucose and salts are selectively reabsorbed as the filtrate moves through the tubule.
concepts.real_example: Urine becoming more concentrated (darker) on a hot day when the body needs to conserve water.
key_points: Kidneys maintain water and ionic balance
Urea is produced in the liver and filtered in the kidneys
Artificial kidney (dialysis) works on the principle of diffusion
